author_headline: Writer & Entrepreneur. I write The Saturday Essay for 200,000+ ambitious people living and working on their own terms.
content:
Nobody cares what you're doing, and that's pretty great news.

Most people act like everyone's paying attention.

Coworkers judging them.
Old friends laughing at their efforts.
Their online audience waiting to pounce.

They get scared to move.
Business ideas get shelved.
Content stays in draft mode.

All to manage the opinions of an audience who isn't paying attention.

The truth is that everyone else is too busy worrying about their own stuff to care about yours.

The embarrassment you're avoiding doesn't even exist.

Once you realize nobody cares, you can finally publish, launch, ask people to buy, and mess around in public.

That's the freedom that let me build my one-person business in public for seven years.

But getting in the game is just step one.

You've still gotta figure out what actually grows a business, and I learned most of that the hard way.

So, I packaged everything I learned for free, and I'm giving it away.

250+ essays, thousands of hours of voice notes, and interviews with successful friends all jammed into a 30-page manifesto on building an eight-figure business.

You can read it free here: https://buff.ly/qyPzps4

author_headline: Founder & Host @ Figuring Out | Building Cüraa by YFL Home | Bestselling Author, Build Don't Talk
content:
Your brain will accept almost any amount of hard work, as long as one condition is met.

It has to be able to see the work making some progress.

Your brain is rarely tracking the difficulty. It is tracking direction: whether today put you closer to something than yesterday did.

When that signal is there, people put up with a lot. When it disappears, even normal effort starts to feel like too much.

Teresa Amabile at Harvard studied this by reading through nearly 12,000 daily diary entries from people at work. The biggest driver of motivation on any given day was the feeling of having made progress on something that mattered.

It beat recognition, incentives, and pressure. Small, visible movement did more for people than almost anything else.

The opposite is also true. Work with no clear end, no way to tell if you are closer than you were last week, slowly stops feeling hard and starts feeling pointless.

You might have felt this in a project that dragged on with no finish line, or in a stretch where you were doing everything right and had no way to know if it was working.

What wears you down in those periods is rarely the workload. It is working without knowing.

Which is why a lot of what people call a discipline problem is really a visibility problem. The ability to work is still there. People just run out of proof that it is going somewhere.

#rajshamani #figuringout

author_headline: Make something people want.
content:
OneCLI (YC S26) lets AI agents do real work in companies without storing passwords.

AI agents are doing real work now, which means they need real credentials: API keys, database passwords, OAuth tokens. Today, teams either hardcode secrets into .env files the agent can read or build one-off proxy hacks. Either way, the agent holds the secret, and an agent that holds a secret can leak it through prompt injection, a malicious MCP server, a compromised dependency, or a pasted log. Secrets management was designed for humans and deterministic software. Autonomous agents that browse untrusted webpages, install packages, and write their own code are a fundamentally different threat model.

OneCLI moves the credential out of the agent. Real secrets live in OneCLI's vault, and agents only ever hold placeholder tokens. One command routes every outbound call from your coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor) or autonomous agent (OpenClaw, Hermes Agent, NanoClaw) through OneCLI, which checks each request against policy, swaps the placeholder for the real credential at the network layer, and forwards it. The agent never touches the secret, so there is nothing to steal, log, or exfiltrate. It runs in a single Docker container, works with anything that can set HTTPS_PROXY, and supports IdP integration and organization-wide policy so teams get full control and visibility over every access their agents make.

OneCLI has passed 320K downloads and +2,500 GitHub stars, ships as the default credential layer in NanoClaw (30K+ stars), and has an official Bitwarden Agent Access SDK integration, with a 1Password integration.

Lock down your agents' credentials: https://onecli.sh

Congrats on the launch, Jonathan Haim Fishner and Guy Ben-Aharon!

🚀 https://lnkd.in/gk8pcu3j

author_headline: 53K • Industrial Trainee • Barclays • CA Finalist • Marketing • Top 2% • Founder @ Capitalized • B.com(H)
content:
i made my first income of ₹500 seven years ago.

it was from giving home tuitions after school, and it completely changed me.

back then where asking my dad for pocket money every time i wanted to grab coffee with friends just felt awkward. he never once made me feel guilty about expenses, but there was this sudden urge inside me - i wanted my own financial independence.

when that first note landed on my hands, i was on cloud nine because i knew i earned it myself.

disclaimer: i'm NOT saying these are the only ways to earn, or that you should sacrifice your studies just to make a quick buck. but if you have a bit of extra free time and want some personal spending money without relying on your parents, these are super doable options.

here are 5 realistic paths:

🔸 remote micro-internships - stop waiting for campus placement season. platforms like internshala, linkedin, and wellfound are packed with short-term, remote internships in market research, lead generation, and operations. taking up 2-3 small remote stints during college builds a killer resume before you even graduate.

🔸 ghostwriting - if you have strong writing skills, pitch ghostwriting, post-drafting, or newsletter management to startup founders and busy professionals. another massive, underrated space right now is curriculum and course writing for ed-tech platforms. send a few free samples as a mini case study to showcase your tone.

🔸 virtual tuitions - you don't need a teaching degree, just solid fundamentals. if you aced accounting, coding, or math, host 1-on-1 online sessions for school students. using your own network on whatsapp/instagram or listing yourself on tutoring portals can easily fetch your first few paid clients.

🔸 short/long form video editing - creators and brands are constantly hunting for people who can edit clean, high-retention reels and shorts. learning capcut, filmora, premiere pro, or daVinci takes literally a week of dedicated youtube tutorials. build a 30-second sample portfolio, slide into creator dms, and offer a free trial edit to lock them in.

🔸 virtual assistant & founder support - founders are drowning in admin work - managing calendars, sorting emails, scraping leads, and organizing spreadsheets. reaching out to executives as a remote virtual assistant for 5-10 hours a week is one of the most consistent ways to secure a steady monthly stipend.

at the end of the day, earning your own money is a great feeling.

you stop waiting for permission and start building your own confidence.

author_headline: Make something people want.
content:
Robots can do karate kicks, cut carrots, and bake bread in demo videos. But how capable are they, really?

Robocurve measures how good AI and robots are in the physical world, from making a sandwich to building data centers.

Frontier labs are targeting general-purpose robotics by 2028, yet the field of robotics evals barely exists. Labs evaluate in-house, and no independent group has stepped in to run continuous benchmarking as a service. The result is that no one actually knows how good anyone else is, or where the real frontier sits.

Robocurve is scaling the field of robotics evals as fast as possible. They're releasing Inspect Robots, an open-source evaluation framework to evaluate any AI model on any robot, on any benchmark, real-world or simulation. Inspect Robots works with 40 VLAs (π0.5, GR00T N1.7, MolmoAct2 and more via XPolicyLab) and 260+ frontier LLMs on day one. They're using Inspect Robots to evaluate Claude Fable 5 on real robots and are building out multiple decision-relevant benchmarks.

Whoever builds the trusted measure of robot capability becomes the reference everyone relies on. METR, Epoch, and Artificial Analysis have done this for LLMs. No one has done it for physical AI, yet. Robocurve is a Public Benefit Corporation dedicated to providing independent analysis of robotics capabilities.

Founders Jay C. and Aris Zhu combine backgrounds in AI evals and robotics. Jay has experience in AI safety and evals (UK AISI, MATS, Inspect Evals, AWS Bedrock), while Aris has robotics experience from Amazon Robotics, Amazon AGI Lab, Yondu Robotics (YC W24), and Harvard's Computational Robotics Lab, with research published in IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters.

Congrats on the launch, Jay and Aris!

🚀 https://lnkd.in/gwGTm-Wf

author_headline: Founder & CEO of Club Peppermint | Building a Luxury 80s Inspired Start Up Fashion Brand ⚡️🏆 Raising SEIS
content:
Things I wish I knew EARLY about business 😅😳

I've been an entrepreneur since I was 18, and let's just say I learnt most of these the hard way.

Advice I'd give ANYONE starting their own company:

1️⃣ Think of a timeline, then double it 😂 Someone told me this when I started building Club Peppermint and I was like nahhh, I'm being realistic. Phah. So wrong 💀 The timeline in your head never allows for s*** to happen. And it WILL. Aim for your date, but build in a few months of buffer.

2️⃣ Your numbers are your BIGGEST superpower - Strip any business back and it's simple. Positive money = a business that survives. CB Insights analysed 431 failed startups and running out of capital showed up in 70% of them. Know your margins, your cash position, what's coming in and out. And don't let VAT bite you 😅

3️⃣ The quickest way to FAIL is to give up - You can pivot, change suppliers, redo the pricing, scrap the whole strategy. I've done all of it. Every problem I've hit had a way through, but only because I stayed in long enough to find it. The moment you decide it's over is the only point it actually is 🤍

4️⃣ You don't have to launch just once - Airbnb launched THREE times?!?! Their SXSW launch in 2008 got two bookings, one was the founder himself 😭 They relaunched at the DNC, got press, still nothing. Broke and in debt, they sold cereal boxes to survive. Third time it worked. So if your launch flops, that's not the verdict. Launch until someone notices 👀

5️⃣ Done beats perfect - I lost so much time waiting for things to be exactly right. The perfect website, the perfect photos. Nobody cares about the details you're agonising over as much as you do. You learn ten times faster from something live and slightly wrong than something perfect sat in drafts. Ship it, fix it after 🚀

❓What would YOU add to this list? let me know in the comments!!! 👇

♻️ Repost if you learnt something!!!

#startup #founder #entrepreneur #buildinginpublic #clubpeppermint

author_headline: Principal Software Engineer @Atlassian| Ex-Sr. Engineer @Microsoft || Sharing insights on SW Engineering, Career Growth & Interview Preparation
content:
A rate limiter sounds simple to create until you look at the exact time window. The requirement says: "Allow 100 requests per minute."

A user sends 100 requests at 11:59:59, then 100 more at 12:00:01. That is 200 requests in 2 seconds, but the limiter says it is fine.

So where is the bug? The problem is the algorithm you chose.

[1] Fixed window allows boundary bursts

A fixed window limiter counts requests inside a fixed calendar window.

For example:

→ 11:59 minute window: 100 requests allowed
→ 12:00 minute window: 100 requests allowed

So when the clock moves to the next minute, the counter resets. The user did not break the limiter. They used the edge of the window.

This design is cheap, fast, and easy to build with Redis. But it allows bursts at boundaries.

Btw, if you're preparing for Senior to Principal-level system design interviews, I've put together 90+ fundamentals like this into a guide.

You can check it out here: puneetpatwari.in

[2] Sliding window fixes the meaning

If the product requirement really means: "Never allow more than 100 requests in any rolling 60-second period"

then fixed window is the wrong choice.

You need a sliding window.

The system keeps recent request timestamps and checks the last 60 seconds before allowing the next request.

So at 12:00:01, the 100 requests from 11:59:59 are still inside the window. The next 100 requests get blocked.

More accurate.
More expensive.

That is the tradeoff.

[3] Token bucket is often the practical middle ground

Sometimes short bursts are acceptable, but sustained abuse is not.

That is where token bucket works well.
→ Bucket refills at a steady rate
→ Each request consumes one token
→ If tokens are available, allow
→ If bucket is empty, reject or delay

This gives users some burst capacity while still controlling average traffic over time.

[4] The real interview answer

A good answer is not: "Use Redis."

A good answer is: "What behavior do we actually want?"

If boundary bursts are acceptable, fixed window is fine. If strict rolling limits matter, use sliding window. If controlled bursts are okay, use token bucket.

A rate limiter is not just a counter.
It is a traffic contract.

If you define the contract poorly, the system can be technically correct and still wrong for production.

author_headline: मार्केटिंग @magicpin | Branding and Advertising | Content Writing | Marketing | IIM Indore
content:
A broker asked me something during my last apartment hunt that I hadn't heard even two years ago.

"Would you also like to see the unfurnished option?"

I said yes, mostly out of curiosity.

The furnished apartment was ₹10,000 more, every month.

The unfurnished one was bare and cold.

I sat with that number for a while and then did what a marketer eventually does with any number. I broke the deal apart.

The apartment was never the expensive part. The furniture inside it was.

A landlord buys a sofa once. Every tenant after pays for it again. Every month, for as long as they stay.

I started calling it the furniture tax. Not a real tax. Just a premium we've accepted for so long, we stopped noticing we were paying it.

But bundles are breaking apart everywhere. Music unbundled into playlists. Cars unbundled into rides. Software stopped selling itself as one fixed box.
Homes are unbundling too.

You can choose the apartment on its own merit now. Better light, better location, better rent. And build everything inside it, piece by piece, on your own terms.

That's what more renters around me are doing.

Taking the apartment that makes sense on its own. Then renting the bed, the fridge, the washing machine, the wardrobe, through RentoMojo, for a fraction of what the furniture tax would have cost.

The apartment you actually want. The furniture you actually choose.

No bundle deciding either for you.

author_headline: VP of Sales @ Rippling | GTM Advisor
content:
Your idea isn't the problem. How you communicate it is.

In part (2 of 3) of Rippling's Venture Corner with Vanessa Kahkesh, I explain.

Early in my career, when I'd spot a problem and make recommendations to my manager, it almost always turned into a bunch of back-and-forths, ultimately fizzling out with no changes made.

The problem wasn't the ideas,
It was my delivery.

Over time, I figured out how to make recommendations that solicit immediate action, and turned it into a simple framework:

S.P.I.R.A.L.

👉 𝗦𝗶𝘁𝘂𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: Provide context. Your manager may not be as close to the details as you. Keep it clear. Keep it concise.

👉 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺: Make sure you have a real problem. It's your job to decipher "volume vs volume". Meaning is this happening often, or do you just a loud, squeaky wheel? Provide at least two real examples!

👉 𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗰𝘁: How does this problem impact outcomes you care about? Avoid subjective terms like "often, big". Be data-backed.

👉 𝗥𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: This is where you demonstrate your ability to be solution oriented. Make your recommendation clear, but be sure to include alternative options...listing the pros and cons of each.

👉 𝗔𝘀𝗸: Be explicit. What needs to change? Who? How? When?

👉 𝗟𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗲, 𝗙𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁: Don't burry the lead! The TLDR, put this at the top.

Save this.
Use this.

Not only will your recommendations solicit action,
I guarantee you'll stand out from your peers.

Every classroom had that one genius. 😂

Not the topper. The innovator.

He didn't know the formula. He didn't know the concept. But his confidence was on another level.

He confidently said 7² − 6² = 13.

The teacher was speechless. The whole class lost it. 💀

Looking back, that's probably where half our "creative maths" skills came from. 😂

Now, if I feel like challenging my brain, I'd rather do it the right way on Matiks. A quick daily challenge, a mind snap duel or a Sprint Duel is way more fun than making up my own formulas.

author_headline: B.Com (Hons.) @ DU'27 | Personal Branding • Storytelling • Finance | Brand Partnerships
content:
She spent 26 years in the same company.

But that's not what surprised me.

Yesterday, during our visit to Coforge,
I asked one of the employees how long she'd been with the company.

"Twenty-six years."

I couldn't hide my surprise.

So I asked the question that instantly came to my mind.

"What made you stay?"

I expected answers like,
stability, promotions, or work culture.

Instead, she shared something completely different.

There were phases when she thought about switching.

But before making that decision, she would explore.

⭐ A different role.

⭐ A different project.

⭐ A new skill.

She kept reinventing herself before changing her surroundings.

And that's when it clicked.

This isn't a story about spending 26 years in one company.

It's a story about spending 26 years without losing your curiosity.

She didn't stay because nothing changed.

She stayed because she never stopped changing.

Every new role.

Every new project.

Every new skill.

Curiosity kept giving her a reason to stay.

I walked into Coforge expecting to learn about careers.

I walked out with one reminder.

Never let your curiosity retire before you do.

Because careers don't stop growing when opportunities disappear.

They stop growing when curiosity does.

#CareerGrowth #ContinuousLearning #Curiosity #CorporateLife #Coforge

author_headline: Building my brand while teaching you to build yours ⭐️
content:
AI hasn't made content creation harder.

it's made standing out harder.

because now anyone can write a clean, structured, well-hooked post in 30 seconds.

and they do every single day
and most of it disappears into the feed without leaving a mark.

not because it's badly written but because it has nothing to say.

I've been thinking about this a lot lately.

the creators I actually follow, read, remember, they're not the most polished.

⭐️ they're the most specific.

⭐️ they talk about things that happened to them.

⭐️ they have opinions that make me go oh I've never thought about it that way.

⭐️ they sound like a person, not a content machine.

AI can write for you.
it cannot live your life for you.

and your life is the only content strategy that actually can't be copied.

what do you think AI will never be able to replace about you specifically?
